concerns.text: FR-005 states 'If VIF ≥5 for any predictor, the model MUST be flagged as having multicollinearity concerns and alternative methods...MUST be considered.' However, braid index ≤ crossing number is a KNOWN mathematical constraint (not an empirical observation). VIF will be high by design. This treats an expected mathematical consequence as a problem to fix rather than acknowledging it as inherent to the predictor structure.

The spec acknowledges 'braid index ≤ crossing number is a known mathematical constraint, not an empirical finding' (Assumptions) and that joint regression answers 'a variance partitioning question rather than independent predictive power' (FR-005). However, the research question ('To what extent do crossing number and braid index jointly explain variation...') presupposes independent explanatory variables when they are mathematically constrained. The variance partitioning question itself may be ill-posed given the definitional relationship between predictors.

FR-005 and SC-002 specify comparing regression models using R², AIC/BIC, MAE. For census data with mathematically constrained predictors, these metrics describe fit to the finite dataset but cannot support claims about 'variance explained' in any meaningful statistical sense. The spec acknowledges this ('Descriptive Interpretation: R²...are interpreted as variance explained in this finite dataset') but the terminology may still invite misinterpretation.
Assumptions section contains redundant citations: 'source: OEIS A002863, https://oeis.org/A002863' appears three times in the same paragraph. Consolidate to single reference.
